title: Environmental Justice Mapping Tool
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive p5.js MicroSim where students correlate pollution sources, income levels, health outcomes, and green space across a fictional city to identify patterns of environmental injustice.

learningObjectives: Correlate locations of environmental hazards with demographic data to identify patterns of environmental injustice
Analyze how income, race, pollution exposure, and health outcomes are spatially related
Evaluate environmental justice claims using quantitative evidence
Propose evidence-based policy solutions to address environmental inequities
prerequisites: Basic understanding of pollution types and sources

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Layered map exploration with stakeholder perspective switching
keyQuestions: What spatial patterns emerge when pollution and income layers are viewed together?
Why do environmental hazards tend to cluster in low-income neighborhoods?
How does green space access correlate with income and health outcomes?
What policies could reduce the environmental justice disparities shown on the map?
Whose perspective matters most when making decisions about facility placement?
commonMisconceptions: Environmental hazards are randomly distributed across communities
Low-income communities choose to live near pollution sources voluntarily
Environmental justice is only about race, not about income or other factors
assessmentOpportunities: District comparison data analysis with quantitative evidence
Mock city council policy proposal presentation
